iris-mlp.placardText:
The Micro-Sprite is the smallest work in the gallery, but also one of the clearest. This model's job is simple and real: given four measurements of an iris flower — sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width — it predicts which of three species the flower belongs to. What you are looking at is the entire trained model: 67 learned numbers arranged into a compact 9×8 field.

The mosaic itself reveals the model's architecture. The upper block corresponds to the hidden layer, where eight neurons each learn a different combination of botanical features; the lower block is the output layer, where those learned features are recombined into the final three-way classification. Because the model is so small, the image has an almost diagrammatic clarity. You can see the separation between stages of thought: first, transform measurements into internal features; second, turn those features into a decision.

There are patterns worth noticing here. The hidden-layer weights often organize themselves into columns with contrasting warm and cool tones, which means the model is learning to push some measurements together and others apart. In the iris dataset, petal measurements are especially informative, so stronger contrasts tend to appear where the model has found a sharp way to separate species that look superficially similar. The output layer is more polarized still: once the hidden features are learned, the final layer becomes a strong vote for one species and against the others.

What can this visualization teach us? First, that useful machine learning models do not have to be enormous. Second, that even a tiny network has structure: it is not random noise, but a staged set of learned relationships. The Micro-Sprite will not tell you the exact verbal rule the classifier learned, but it does let you see that the model's reasoning is compact, layered, and surprisingly legible.

layerAnnotations.fc1.weight: Eight learned questions about petal and sepal geometry — each column a different way of seeing a flower.
layerAnnotations.fc1.bias: Threshold offsets — how much evidence each hidden neuron needs before it activates.
layerAnnotations.fc2.weight: The final vote: three rows mapping eight features to three species. Warm means yes; cool means no.
layerAnnotations.fc2.bias: Decision thresholds for each species prediction.

mnist-8.placardText:
The Portrait is a model that recognizes handwritten digits. Its task is to look at a 28×28 grayscale image and decide whether it depicts a 0, 1, 2, or any other digit through 9. The mosaic gathers all 5,994 learned weights of that classifier into a single image, so what looks at first like abstract patterning is in fact the frozen memory of a machine that has learned how people write numbers.

Several visual patterns in the mosaic relate directly to what the model does. Near the top are the convolutional filters: small repeating motifs that often show diagonals, bars, and directional contrasts. That is exactly what we should expect from a handwriting recognizer. Digits are made from strokes, bends, and crossings, so the first stage of the model learns simple detectors for edges and orientations. Deeper in the mosaic, the patterns become denser and less obviously geometric. Those later filters are no longer detecting a single stroke; they are combining strokes into loops, corners, and fragments of shapes that distinguish, say, an 8 from a 3 or a 7 from a 1.

At the bottom sits the dense classification layer, whose texture is grainier and more statistical. By this stage the model is no longer preserving the spatial elegance of a handwritten mark; it is weighing evidence. The image shifts from something almost textile-like to something more like a field of probabilities. This is a useful lesson in itself: as information moves through the network, it becomes less pictorial and more abstract.

What can we learn from the visualization? Not how to read out the model's exact rule in plain English, but something arguably more important: that the model has a hierarchy. Early regions of the mosaic encode seeing; later regions encode judging. The Portrait teaches that even a modest vision model is built from stages of abstraction, and the mosaic lets you watch that transition happen in a single frame.

layerAnnotations.Parameter5: Eight 5×5 convolutional kernels — the model's first attempt to see. Each filter has learned to detect a different oriented edge or gradient.
layerAnnotations.Parameter6: Bias values for the first conv layer — subtle threshold adjustments.
layerAnnotations.Parameter87: Sixteen 5×5 filters operating on eight input channels — 3,200 weights that combine simple edges into curves, corners, and junctions.
layerAnnotations.Parameter88: Bias values for the second conv layer.
layerAnnotations.Parameter193: The dense classification layer — 2,560 weights mapping spatial features into digit identity. The texture here is noise-like because spatial structure has been abstracted away.
layerAnnotations.Parameter194: Output bias — ten values, one per digit, encoding the model's prior expectation for each class.

cifar10-tiny.placardText:
The Landscape is the largest and most intricate work in the gallery because it comes from the most ambitious model of the three. This network is trained to classify small color photographs from CIFAR-10 into categories like airplane, automobile, bird, cat, deer, dog, frog, horse, ship, and truck. In other words, it must look at a noisy 32×32 color image and infer what kind of object or scene it contains. The mosaic gathers 14,410 learned weights into a single image, and its size alone tells you something: this task requires a richer visual vocabulary than flowers or digits.

Patterns in the mosaic are not random decoration; they mirror the architecture. The upper regions contain the earliest convolutional filters, which are the only part of the model that sees raw color channels directly. These areas often look sharper, brighter, and more contrast-heavy because the model is learning elementary visual primitives: color transitions, edges, gradients, and small texture fragments. Deeper layers become denser and more stratified. Their patterns are harder to interpret by eye, but that difficulty is meaningful: the model has moved beyond raw pixels and is now combining simple cues into more complex parts, the visual ingredients of wheels, fur, wings, and horizon lines.

The largest single region in the mosaic belongs to the dense layer. It appears more like a stippled field than an image of anything in particular, and that is exactly right. At this stage the model is no longer preserving a spatial map of the photo; it is compressing many local visual signals into a small set of abstract features and using them to cast a categorical vote. The shift from structured kernels to near-pointillist grain is the shift from seeing to deciding.

What can this visualization teach us? It shows, very concretely, where the model's capacity lives and how a vision system distributes its labor. Early layers are interpretable because they learn reusable visual atoms. Later layers are harder to decode because they are solving the harder part of the problem: composition and judgment. The Landscape reminds us that opacity in machine learning often arrives not from chaos, but from success — the better the network becomes at combining simple cues into useful abstractions, the less its internal pictures resemble the world they classify.

layerAnnotations.conv1.weight: Eight 3×3 color filters — the model's retinas. Each kernel learns to detect a different color-edge combination in the raw photograph.
layerAnnotations.conv1.bias: Bias for the first conv layer.
layerAnnotations.conv2.weight: Sixteen filters combining first-layer edges into textures and corners — 1,152 weights of increasing visual abstraction.
layerAnnotations.conv2.bias: Bias for the second conv layer.
layerAnnotations.conv3.weight: Thirty-two deep filters — 4,608 weights encoding parts of objects: curves of wings, straight edges of buildings, fur textures of animals.
layerAnnotations.conv3.bias: Bias for the third conv layer.
layerAnnotations.fc1.weight: The dense mapping — 8,192 weights compressing spatial features into 16 abstract category signals. A pointillist canvas of learned associations.
layerAnnotations.fc2.weight: The final classifier — 160 weights casting ten votes, one per object category.
layerAnnotations.fc2.bias: Output bias — prior expectations for each of the ten CIFAR-10 categories.
